content.text: Apple's Vision framework runs image analysis models entirely on-device, with no network call and no data leaving the phone. Combined with Claude, it enables a powerful hybrid pattern: Vision classifies what is in an image cheaply and privately, then Claude reasons about those labels with natural-language context. This section covers how Vision's classification pipeline works, how to drive it from Swift, and how to structure the result for a prompt to Claude.

attrs.alt: Apple Vision framework on-device image classification pipeline: Input → VNImageRequestHandler → VNClassifyImageRequest → Results, with thread safety pattern and ToiletAlarm Vision-to-Claude flow

content.text: Vision uses a three-object model. A
subclass describes the analysis to perform. Calling
executes the model synchronously on the calling thread. Results come back as
subclass instances attached to the request.
For image classification specifically, the request type is
string (a human-readable label such as "cat" or "outdoor") and a
float from 0 to 1. The built-in classification model covers thousands of concepts and returns them sorted by confidence, highest first.
The key design choices here: the call runs on a serial background queue because
is synchronous and blocks its thread. The orientation is taken from
so Vision does not misread a rotated JPEG. Omitting this causes the model to see a sideways image and return incorrect labels for photos taken in portrait mode.
The completion-handler style above can be wrapped with
to produce an async function that composes naturally with a
Building the Claude Prompt from Labels

The point of Vision is to reduce what Claude needs to do. Rather than sending a base64-encoded image to Claude's vision API — which costs more tokens and requires sending photo data off-device — Vision produces a short label list that Claude can reason about in a simple text prompt. This is the ToiletAlarm pattern: local classification first, then targeted reasoning.
Vision provides many request types beyond classification. The same handler-and-request pattern applies to all of them:
Send label list as text context
Send OCR'd text for summarization or Q&A
Report face count and bounding rects to Claude
Decode barcode payload, let Claude look up product details
Similarity search — no direct Claude use, feeds retrieval
, frames arrive faster than Vision can process them. Use a flag or a dedicated serial queue to drop frames while a request is running:
guard is not thread-safe as written — in production, use
or an actor. For a ToiletAlarm-style pattern where you want to classify once on user tap rather than continuously, use a still capture instead of a live feed.

The right threshold depends on the prompt design. A threshold that is too low sends noise labels like "light" or "color" that add no useful context and inflate the prompt. A threshold that is too high might filter out the single relevant label for an ambiguous scene.
: Only near-certain labels. Good for document scanning (text, receipt) where false positives are costly.
: Balanced. Good for general scene description prompts where you want Claude to know the top candidates.
: Broad sweep. Useful when you let Claude decide which labels are relevant by including all of them in the prompt.

In practice, capping the result count at 5–8 labels avoids bloating the prompt regardless of threshold. The classification model returns hundreds of observations for most images; truncating after the top few is almost always the right call.
The Vision framework works in the simulator. Use
on bundled test images in the unit test target to get deterministic label sets. To decouple tests from the real classifier, define a protocol:
Calling perform on the main thread.
is synchronous and blocks. On the main thread this freezes the UI for the duration of model inference (typically 50–200ms on A-series chips, longer on older hardware).
JPEG photos from the camera store rotation metadata in EXIF. Without passing the correct
, Vision processes the raw bytes in the default orientation and produces mismatched labels for portrait photos.
The model returns hundreds of labels including "font", "black", "white", "pattern", and other generic descriptors. Including these in the Claude prompt wastes tokens and dilutes the useful context.
Creating a new handler and request for every live frame.
While the objects are lightweight, doing this without frame-dropping causes a backlog of queued requests. Process one frame at a time with the
If the dominant content is printed text (a receipt, a sign, a book page),
will produce much more useful content for a Claude prompt than the general classifier, which will just return labels like "text", "font", and "document".
runs a multi-thousand-category model on-device in under 200ms, returning sorted

objects with identifier and confidence. Run it on a serial background queue, pass the correct EXIF orientation, filter observations above a confidence threshold (0.5 is a practical starting point), cap at 5–8 results, and format them as a compact label list in the Claude prompt. This pattern keeps photo data on-device, reduces Claude API costs, and makes responses faster by giving Claude precise structured context rather than asking it to interpret a raw image.
